name: Trina Vertex S+ 430W
notes: Optimal engineering choice for short strings (e.g., 3 to 6 panels). The 144-cell layout yields unusually high voltage for its size, establishing a massive safety margin above Victron MPPT startup thresholds. Dual glass provides excellent long-term durability.
engineering_notes: High voltage (51.4V) ensures early inverter wake-up, making it excellent for short strings. However, long strings risk exceeding 450V limits in cold weather. Dual glass provides superior durability, fire rating, and microcrack resistance.

name: Trina Vertex S+ 435W
notes: Higher power bin of the Vertex S+. Identical physical footprint. Check max voltage against 8-panel strings in freezing conditions to avoid 450V limits.
engineering_notes: Identical form factor to the 430W. The 51.8V Voc requires strict string sizing in cold climates to avoid over-voltaging MPPTs. Excellent durability from n-type dual-glass construction. Highly regarded for short-string performance.

name: Trina Vertex S+ 440W
notes: Extreme voltage format. Highly recommended for 3-panel arrays (like Garages or Dormers) to guarantee early morning wake-up. **WARNING:** 8 of these in series at -10°C will exceed 450V and destroy Victron RS 450 trackers.
engineering_notes: Extreme 52.2V Voc is perfect for 3-panel arrays on dormers, but demands strict calculation for strings over 7 panels on 450V controllers. Heavy (21.1kg) but robust. Exceptionally high power density for residential roofs.

name: Trina Vertex S+ 445W
notes: The highest bin currently available in the S+ 144-cell footprint. Superb for maximizing wattage on severely space-constrained roofs. Do not use on strings longer than 7 panels on a 450V MPPT.
engineering_notes: Top-tier power bin for the S+ footprint. Superb for tiny roofs. Beware the 52.6V Voc: an 8-panel string at -10°C will easily exceed 450V. Unbeatable degradation resistance thanks to the n-type dual-glass architecture.

name: Trina Vertex S+ 505W (Large)
notes: Commercial format panel. Physically too long for GSE Landscape trays. Highly efficient way to pack 500W+ into portrait arrays while maintaining a healthy voltage profile and keeping current well below Victron limits.
engineering_notes: Commercial format. Lower Voc (40.3V) but high current (15.89A Isc) pairs better with high-current string inverters than voltage-limited MPPTs. Check string wire ampacity ratings carefully due to the high operating current.

name: AIKO NeoStar 2S 460W
notes: Market-leading efficiency using ABC (All Back Contact) technology. No front grid lines results in a premium pure black aesthetic. Excellent power density.
engineering_notes: Premium ABC tech yields stunning all-black aesthetics and massive 23.1% efficiency. Standard 41V Voc is great for long strings. Single-glass build keeps weight manageable despite high output. Highly praised for partial shading performance.

name: AIKO NeoStar 2S 465W
notes: Premium efficiency ABC module. Strong performance, but voltage drops sharply in extreme heat. 3-panel strings will cut it uncomfortably close to the 120V Victron RS 450 startup threshold in summer.
engineering_notes: Market-leading 23.3% efficiency. No front busbars mean exceptional curb appeal. Voltages drop in extreme heat, so ensure short strings meet inverter startup thresholds during summer. A premium choice for space-limited roofs.

name: AIKO NeoStar 2S 470W
notes: The pinnacle of standard-footprint wattage. Use this to cram maximum generation into a small roof space where budget is less of a concern than absolute yield.
engineering_notes: Unparalleled standard-footprint energy density (23.6%). Excellent for maximizing generation on tiny roofs. It commands a premium price but delivers elite yield. Standard 108-cell voltage limitations apply; prioritize for longer strings.

name: AIKO NeoStar 600W (Large)
notes: Massive utility-scale footprint. Will not fit on most residential dormers or complex hips. Over 2.2 meters long. If your roof can physically take it in portrait, it offers incredible power without exceeding standard tracker current limits.
engineering_notes: Massive utility/commercial panel. At nearly 2.3 meters long, it is unsuitable for standard residential roofs. Offers incredible power density and a high 53.99V Voc. Ensure mounting systems are rated for its massive windage area.

name: DMEGC Infinity 450W
notes: A very strong middle-ground option. Offers the durability of double-glass and bifacial cell tech with slightly better voltage profiles than standard Tier 1 competitors.
engineering_notes: Strong mid-range n-type bifacial option. Dual glass provides excellent longevity and fire resistance. The 40.25V Voc makes it highly versatile for longer residential strings. Bifacial gain is minimal on pitched roofs but excellent for flat mounts.

name: DMEGC Infinity 455W
notes: Solid N-Type bifacial panel. High quality build, but 3-panel strings run the risk of dropping below 120V required for RS MPPTs on 30°C+ days.
engineering_notes: Reliable n-type dual-glass module. Highly durable. Standard 40.45V Voc means short strings risk dropping below 120V startup thresholds in hot weather. Excellent build quality and reasonable pricing make it a solid daily driver.

name: DMEGC G12 Infinity RT 515W
notes: ENGINEERING WARNING: Uses larger G12-RT rectangular wafers which achieve wattage by pumping massive current. At 16.5A, this pushes the 18A hard limit of the Victron RS 450 MPPT to the absolute edge. Leaves almost zero safety margin for cloud-edge effects. Must be strictly managed.
engineering_notes: WARNING: Uses large G12-RT rectangular wafers, achieving wattage through massive current (16.5A Isc). Pushes hard limits of many residential MPPTs (like Victron RS 18A limits). Requires strictly checked wire sizing and inverter current-limit matching.

name: Jinko Tiger Neo 430W
notes: Cost-effective industry workhorse. Known for excellent low-light degradation characteristics. The lower Voc (38.9V) makes it excellent for long 8/9 panel strings, but terrible for short 3-panel strings.
engineering_notes: The definitive industry workhorse. N-type tech gives excellent low-light performance. The 38.9V Voc is ideal for long strings (10+ panels) but terrible for small arrays on high-voltage MPPTs. Unbeatable value and proven reliability.

name: Jinko Tiger Neo 440W
notes: Higher wattage Tiger Neo. Retains the standard 108-cell limitations: great for long strings, high risk of voltage-drop failures on arrays with fewer than 4 panels.
engineering_notes: Cost-effective, proven Tiger Neo module. Sits perfectly in the goldilocks zone for standard residential string inverters. Like all standard 108-cell panels, it is prone to voltage-drop issues on very short strings in hot weather.

name: Jinko Tiger Neo 475W (Large)
notes: Slightly shorter commercial format. 120 cell layout gives it a healthier voltage profile than the 108 cell panels, making it versatile for both Victron startup limits and string series limits.
engineering_notes: Versatile 120-cell layout provides a healthier voltage profile (42.4V Voc) than 108-cell variants, bridging the gap between short and long string requirements. Ensure roof dimensions accommodate the 1.9m length. Great price-to-performance ratio.

name: JA Solar JAM54S31 430W
notes: Budget-friendly and ubiquitous. However, it has the lowest Voc of all Tier 1 residential panels. Do not use on any array smaller than 4 panels if utilizing Victron RS series chargers.
engineering_notes: Budget-friendly older PERC module. At 38.09V Voc, it has one of the lowest voltages in its class. Absolutely do not use on arrays smaller than 4 panels with high-startup inverters. Lags behind newer n-type tech.

name: JA Solar JAM54D40 440W
notes: Ubiquitous budget panel. Solid reliability, but the electrical architecture is built for cheap string inverters, not premium DC-coupled Victron setups with high start voltages.
engineering_notes: A solid n-type upgrade from JA's PERC lines. Excellent reliability and cost. The electrical architecture remains tuned for long strings on standard string inverters. Not recommended for premium setups requiring high start voltages on short arrays.

name: JA Solar JAM66S30 505W (Large)
notes: Large format. At almost 2.1 meters long, verify the rafter spans of the roof before committing to this for a GSE in-roof system.
engineering_notes: Very large commercial format panel. At nearly 2.1 meters, it requires careful rafter checking for standard mounting. Provides a healthy 45.72V Voc. Good value for money if the roof can physically fit the massive footprint.

name: Canadian Solar HiKu6 430W
notes: A highly reliable, very standard module. Sits directly in the middle of the pack electrically. Nothing exceptional, but very few flaws.
engineering_notes: Highly reliable middle-of-the-road module. It does not excel in any one extreme but offers practically zero flaws. The 39.2V Voc makes it predictable for standard residential strings. A safe, dependable choice.

name: Canadian Solar HiKu6 440W
notes: Dependable standard format. Excellent choice for the 8-panel NW1 array where high voltage panels would overload the system.
engineering_notes: Dependable workhorse. The modest 39.6V Voc is perfect for maximizing panel counts on a single string without breaching 500V/600V inverter limits. A great choice where high-voltage panels would force multiple strings.

name: Longi Hi-MO 6 430W
notes: Uses proprietary HPBC technology to remove all silver busbars from the front of the cell. Incredibly sleek all-black look for highly visible roofscapes.
engineering_notes: Proprietary HPBC tech removes silver busbars from the front, offering a stunning all-black aesthetic. Standard 39V Voc dictates pairing with low-startup MPPTs for short arrays, or stringing in long runs. Lightweight at 20.8kg.

name: Longi Hi-MO 6 440W
notes: Premium aesthetic HPBC module. Beautiful panel, but standard 108-cell voltage limits mean it should be paired with smaller 250V MPPTs for short arrays.
engineering_notes: Premium aesthetic module. Busbar-free HPBC cells look incredible and offer great efficiency (22.5%). Ensure inverter startup limits are respected, as the 39.4V Voc drops in summer heat. Perfect for highly visible roofs.

name: Longi Hi-MO 5 505W (Large)
notes: Commercial format utilizing older Hi-MO 5 tech. Highly dependable.
engineering_notes: Commercial format panel utilizing older but highly proven Hi-MO 5 technology. Very dependable with a solid 45.6V Voc, avoiding the extreme current issues of newer large-wafer panels. Best suited for commercial flat roofs.
